issues.summary: FIVE primary loops claim the player attention; game-concept says progression is weapon/ammo/mech but no loop has explicit 'primary' designation
issues.detail: Pillar 3 is 'every fight is a build test' (battle + weapon-ammo) and Pillar 1 is 'exploration density' (level + random-encounter). Pillar 4 is 'truth is collection' (npc-terminal). The game-concept 'Moment-to-Moment (30 seconds)' section anchors on battle, but 'Long-Term Progression' lists weapon library FIRST, then mech upgrade, then map knowledge, then story puzzle. SaveLoad protects all of these. There is no single GDD that says 'this is the primary loop; everything else is supporting', so each GDD treats its domain as if it were central. #battle-core-loop Player Fantasy is 'never stuttering + tactical depth self-selectable', #weapon-ammo Player Fantasy is 'I have an arsenal, every build is a new discovery', #level-dungeon Player Fantasy is 'I always look forward to the next room', #npc-terminal Player Fantasy is 'I pieced together part of the truth'. All four are framed as THE point. For solo dev in 1-3 month MVP scope, the lack of an explicit 'Pillar X is the primary loop, others are feeders' makes sprint planning harder and risks the classic 4-pillar-team-output-bloat failure mode where each GDD gets prioritized equally and the MVP slips.
issues.recommendation: Add a single ADR or game-concept update paragraph: 'Primary 30s loop = BATTLE (Pillar 3); Primary 30min loop = EXPLORATION + BUILDS (Pillar 1+3); Primary 3hr loop = COLLECTION (Pillar 4)'. Battle = heartbeat, exploration + builds = session, collection = endgame. Each GDD should then revise its Player Fantasy to reference its role (e.g., npc-terminal Player Fantasy adds 'collection is the endgame that makes every other moment matter').

issues.summary: Auto-mode vs manual-mode is a parallel-progression split: Pillar 3 is satisfied by BOTH paths, so neither path is the 'real' primary
issues.detail: battle-core-loop C-R5 says auto-mode AI picks 'optimal strategy' (highest damage + weakness match). This is mathematically equivalent to a smart manual player. There is no Pillar 3 test for whether auto-mode is LESS Pillar 3-fulfilling than manual mode — both satisfy 'every fight is a build test' (auto by AI testing the build, manual by player testing). game-concept 'Player Type Appeal' marks Explorers + Achievers as core/secondary but the design itself rewards two distinct player types equally. The risk: half the playerbase never engages with the 'every fight is a build test' fantasy in the way the design intends, because they default to auto (anti-pillar says auto is fine, but the Pillar 3 TEST requires build testing).
issues.recommendation: Add to game-concept a Pillar 3 sub-clause: 'Pillar 3 in manual mode = player agency; in auto mode = observing the build work (e.g., AI animates the 'optimal play' with a thinking pose per battle-core-loop Visual/Audio row 14)'. Or: distinguish 'Pillar 3-Strategic' (manual) and 'Pillar 3-Observational' (auto) in pillar mapping table. Without this, the Pillar 3 design test in game-concept ('the boss can be defeated with normal ammo, no need to switch to plasma' → wrong) only tests manual, but the anti-pillar explicitly endorses auto as default. Resolve the test asymmetry.

issues.summary: A manual battle decision point activates 8+ systems simultaneously: input (12+4+2 actions) + battle (4-phase loop) + weapon×ammo (9 builds) + HUD (14 elements, 6 always on) + camera (overhead rig) + state (BATTLE badge) + save (autosave on victory) + encounter (count updated)
issues.detail: hud.md C-R4 lists 7 always-visible elements during battle (state badge, mode badge, HP+4 parts, weapon slots, current ammo, enemy HP, turn phase) — that's 7+ Battle-only systems. player-input GDD Battle action set is 12+4+2=18 actions. battle-core-loop Player Fantasy references HP/武器/弹药/模式/状态徽章/Build preview simultaneously ('HUD 切到战斗布局（HP 条 / 武器 / 弹药 / 模式）'). #7 battle-core-loop AC-3 says '按 Q 切弹药 THEN 弹药切换但不消耗回合', #7 AC-4/5 say '按 1/2/3 立即攻击', and #7 AC-7/8 say 'A 键切模式不打断'. Per 玩家输入 G-F4 'every keypress gets SOME visual response', that means 18 actions × visual feedback + HUD updates + battle phase state + save trigger on victory all running on the same frame. The HUD F1 budget is 1.95-3.5ms, transition latency is 9-16.5ms, frame budget is 16.6ms — 4-5ms slack remaining. One miss = dropped frame. This is the classic 60 FPS game where 'too many simultaneous systems' bites. The original task brief called this 'cognitive load risk' — confirmed.
issues.recommendation: Add an explicit 'Battle decision frame budget' calculation to battle-core-loop (analogous to game-state-machine F1 transition budget): enumerate every system that fires in PLAYER_INPUT frame, sum worst-case, assert ≤ 16.6ms. If > 16.6ms, move 'secondary feedback' (e.g., weapon slot pulse HUD F4 = 0.5Hz animation) to next frame or batch with other animations. Defer 1/2/3 attack visual feedback chain (HUD weapon icon highlight per #2 G-H-3, build_damage_preview update, camera shake request, damage number floating) to a 'render queue' so they don't fight for the same frame.

issues.summary: HUD's 14 elements per task brief is contradicted by the GDD's actual 17-element table — and they're rendered EVERY frame, not just when visible
issues.detail: task brief says '14 elements' but hud.md table 14-elements section actually lists 17 rows (state badge, mode badge, player HP, mech 4 parts, weapon slots 0/1/2, current weapon/ammo, enemy HP, enemy 4 parts [BOSS-only], turn phase, encounter counter, damage numbers, loot popup, pickup popup, terminal content box, fragment unlock, chapter summary, 'press E interact' prompt). F1 budget is 1.95-3.5ms for 14, but the table is 17. Per C-R5 'HUD does not hold game state, only consumes state: Dictionary' + '每帧推送', every system pushes per frame: state machine pushes state_changed, battle pushes battle_state, weapon pushes inventory_state, level pushes room_info, encounter pushes count, npc pushes terminal_state, player-input pushes mode, collision pushes interactable. That's 8+ dicts pushed per frame. On a 60 FPS 16.6ms budget this is fine for code but creates an 'always-everything-on-screen' problem: even when in BATTLE, the encounter counter (per table 'EXPLORATION only') still has code paths ready, and HUD's 17th element 'press E' is irrelevant in BATTLE (no interactables in BATTLE state per collision.md state table). Players will read ALL 17 by reflex even when 8 are dark. The attention budget is paid whether elements are visible or not.
issues.recommendation: Update hud.md to commit to C-R4 'priority P1 always-visible' as a strict 4-5 element subset (state badge, mode badge, HP+parts, weapon slots, ammo). Mark the other 12+ as 'triggered-only' (only rendered/updated on the event that needs them) instead of being 'always rendered, just not visible'. This is a major refactor but a real cognitive-load fix. Also fix the 14 vs 17 inconsistency in the GDD (use 14 if it's the MVP, defer 3 to VS).

issues.summary: Exploration moment has 6 simultaneously active systems + 1 not-quite-save-loop, but the design is mostly clean (no dominant strategy concern)
issues.detail: task brief lists: movement (input) + collision (input) + camera + encounter tile check + INTERACTABLE hint + HUD 14 elements + autosave trigger. Actually 7. Compared to battle moment (8+), this is BETTER because: (1) HUD encounter counter is just a number, low attention, (2) autosave only fires on room change (not every tile), (3) camera is passive follow, (4) no build/decision to make. The exception: 'autosave trigger' itself is a system-with-side-effects. save-load.md C-R3 says autosave on 'enter_new_room' but level-dungeon.md OQ-7 OQ says room-completion is 'player leaves the room through a Door/TransitionZone'. So autosave fires when player exits room 1 to room 2 = 'EXPLORATION still active, save happens, HUD toast appears'. Per hud.md C-R4 toast is on-demand, not always-visible, so it doesn't permanently compete for attention. Clean.
issues.recommendation: No action. This scenario is healthy. Document the 'exploration = lighter cognitive load than battle' as a designed Pillar 1 outcome (vs battle = Pillar 3 'designed-to-be-dense').

issues.summary: Auto-mode trivializes manual-mode's Pillar 3 promise AND encounter tile re-triggerable (Pillar 1 design) creates a 'farm optimal' loop that breaks difficulty curve
issues.detail: battle-core-loop C-R5 + F3 say auto-mode AI is: (1) use repair_kit if HP≤30%, (2) defend if enemy one-shots player, (3) max-damage combo, (4) weakness-match priority+1. This IS the optimal play. There is no 'auto-mode disadvantage' documented in any GDD. So manual mode's Pillar 3 promise ('every fight is a build test') becomes vacuous: the auto AI does the build test for you. Combined with random-encounter.md C-R3 'tile cooldown resets when player leaves' + E7 'boss tile re-triggerable but Pillar 1 design intentional' (per OQ-3: '刷怪是有意的'), the player can: (a) turn on auto, (b) walk tile-cooldown loop 25 times for 25 easy wins, (c) build up resources with zero Pillar 3 engagement. resource-data.md confirms 25 ENCOUNTER tiles per chapter, 80% drop rate, ammo pickup 5/15/50 per enemy. The dominant strategy is: auto-mode + tile re-trigger = infinite ammo, infinite XP (no XP cap documented, so no catch-up mechanic), no Pillar 3 engagement. Anti-pillar 'NOT 强制手动' explicitly endorses this, so the design AS IS creates a perfectly valid 'I beat the game without thinking' path. Whether this is a BLOCKER depends on whether Pillar 3 is a hard requirement or aspirational.
issues.recommendation: Two options, both addressable: (1) HARDEN Pillar 3 — add 'Pillar 3 is REQUIRED for chapter completion': e.g., boss encounters only spawn in manual mode (auto-mode triggers flee-equivalent), or auto-mode yields 50% XP/loot, or the truth-fragment-unlock chain (Pillar 4) requires manual victory on at least 3 encounters. (2) SOFTEN Pillar 3 — reframe Pillar 3 as 'available but not required' and add a 'Pillar 5: Comfort' = 'auto-mode is a first-class path, not a workaround'. Recommend option 2: add Pillar 5 to game-concept and reframe Pillar 3 design test to 'when player wants to engage, the build test is there'. This matches anti-pillar reality better. Document the choice in a new ADR or update game-concept Pillar section.

issues.summary: Default build (laser 20 × normal 1.0 = 20 dmg) vs optimal build (missile 50 × plasma 1.3 = 65 dmg) is 3.25× — but resource-data C-R2 says 'min_damage ≤ max_damage ≤ 200' is the production range, so a 200-dmg weapon × 2.0× ammo × 3.0× crit = 1200 theoretical max which violates BOSS 500 HP design
issues.detail: resource-data.md damage_ceiling_analysis: '理论上限（满值 × 满值 × 满值） 200 × 2.0 × 3.0 = 1200' BUT BOSS max_hp = 500. The BOSS 1-shot-on-crit case is implicit. resource-data.md then says '最大 build (195) 击杀 BOSS (500) = 3 击' but this is a 195-dmg build (50×1.3×3.0=195), not the 1200 theoretical max. The 1200 max exists in code as a valid resource (`@export_range(1, 200)` × `@export_range(0.5, 2.0)` × `@export_range(1.0, 3.0)`) but isn't achievable in normal play. weapon-ammo.md F1 says '16 (min: 20×0.8) to 104 (max: 80×1.3)' — production range is 16-104, not 8-480 as battle-core-loop F1 claims ('Output Range: 8 (min) to 480 (max for BOSS = 480)') and NOT 1200 as resource-data says. THREE GDDs disagree on damage bounds. The MVP-default build 20-dmg vs best-build 65-dmg is 3.25× spread which IS enough to matter (per #1 AC-5b 'build 跳跃 3.25×'). But a 'player discovers a 200-dmg weapon × 2.0× ammo build' would one-shot every enemy including BOSS, and resource-data.md allows this combination (no cross-field validator that flags 'final_damage > BOSS_HP'). The 0.05 accuracy floor + 1.0 floor on crit are also unenforced: a weapon with accuracy 0.05 + crit_chance 0.0 + crit_multiplier 1.0 + min_damage 1 + ammo_mult 0.5 = 'perma-weak weapon that almost never hits and does 0 damage on hit' (would hit the minimum-1 rule, so 1-damage-per-hit weapon, 5% accuracy, useless). This is below the 'dominant strategy' threshold but it's 'worst-than-missable'.
issues.recommendation: Cross-doc reconcile damage bounds: (a) Battle #7 F1 says 8-480, Weapon-Ammo #11 F1 says 16-104, Resource #1 says 8-1200. Pick ONE canonical range per the MVP production target ('普通 30-50 HP 普通敌人, 2-3 击, default build'). Suggest canonical: 20-80 base × 0.5-2.0 ammo × 1.0-3.0 crit = MIN 10, MAX 480. (b) Add to resource-data.md a 'build validity' rule: 'no weapon-ammo combination may produce final_damage > 500 (= BOSS_HP cap)' OR 'no combination may produce final_damage > 2x enemy's max_hp on a crit' (the latter is a soft check at battle time, not at design time). Either: cap weapon damage at 80 in practice (drop 200 to 80 ceiling), or add a `boss_immune_to_one_shot` flag. (c) Add a 'build floor' check: weapons that mathematically cannot defeat a normal enemy in <5 turns are flagged as 'junk-tier' and given a `tier: junk` annotation in the codex.

issues.summary: Autosave frequency (per room + per victory) + encounter tile re-trigger makes SaveLoad a 'panic button' that the design doesn't price
issues.detail: save-load.md F1: autosave on enter_new_chapter, enter_new_room, battle_victory = 30+ autosaves per chapter. Combined with random-encounter.md C-R3 'tile re-triggerable on leave' + '刷怪是有意的' OQ, a player can: (a) walk into tile, lose battle, autosave was BEFORE battle so no save-state loss, (b) re-enter, win, autosave. Per battle-core-loop AC-17 'defeat → replace(TITLE)', death = full restart. So the death-punishment is 'lose session, not lose saves'. save-load.md C-R1 '单 autosave slot + 3 manual slots', F5 manual save during EXPLORATION. So a player who F5-saves before a tough encounter, loses, can F9-load the F5. The 'no permanent death' anti-pillar endorses this. But there's no GDD that prices this: how often SHOULD the player be expected to F5-save-scum? If Pillar 3 is the loop, save-scumming trivializes it. If Pillar 1/4 are the loop, save-scumming is fine. The 4-pillar model says all 4 are core — so this is a real ambiguity.
issues.recommendation: Decide and document: is save-scumming allowed, encouraged, or restricted? Three options: (1) 'Free save-scum' (status quo, anti-pillar compatible, Pillar 3 may be undermined); (2) 'Ironman mode only' (add a game-mode toggle, MVP single mode); (3) 'SaveScum disincentive' — F5/F9 use 1 manual slot (the autosave is sacred), so player has 3 chances per chapter, not infinite. Recommend (3) is implicit already in save-load.md C-R1 '3 manual slots' — document this as a deliberate design: '3 manual slots = 3 'rewinds' per chapter'. Add a tutorial/UX hint: 'F5 = bookmark this moment; you have 3 bookmarks. Use them on tough fights.'

issues.summary: Ammo economy has INFINITE source (tile re-trigger → infinite ammo) and no sink (ammo not consumed in battle per #11 E7) — runs away
issues.detail: weapon-ammo.md E7 + OQ-2 confirm ammo is NOT consumed in battle — it's a 'loadout preference'. So ammo only flows ONE WAY: pickup → inventory. Sources: encounter tile re-trigger (random-encounter.md C-R3) + level exploration pickups + boss drops. Sinks: NONE documented. resource-data.md AmmoData.stack_size = 99 (cap). weapon-ammo.md F5: 99 cap with 'lost loot' overflow. So ammo accumulates to 99 of each type, then nothing. The ammo economy is: 'infinite source, no sink, max-99 inventory per type, no consumption'. This is by design (per OQ-2 'ammo = preference, not resource'). But it has a hidden side-effect: the ammo system becomes DECORATIVE — ammo choice doesn't cost the player anything. weapon-ammo.md Player Fantasy 'every build is a new discovery' is undermined because there's no cost to trying all 9 builds. The Pillar 3 'build test' collapses: try all 9 = 0 consequence = no build mastery = no Pillar 3. This is the SAME issue as 3c-1 in different form.
issues.recommendation: Document the no-consumption design as a deliberate choice in game-concept (move from OQ-2 to a 'Resource Economy' section) and align Pillar 3 with it: 'Pillar 3 = low-cost experimentation via no-consumption ammo; Pillar 3 test =

(low cost = encourages trying)'. OR re-introduce a small ammo sink: 'each attack consumes 1 ammo; cap 99; encounter tile re-trigger capped at 3 times per tile'. The current '0 cost' design should be either explicitly endorsed as a Pillar 3 design choice OR fixed with a small consumption rule. Either path is fine; the gap is undeciding.

issues.summary: Weapon economy: 3 slots + 20 backpack = 23 total. Sources: drops (10% grunt, 40% elite, 100% boss = ~6-12 weapons per chapter per F1). Sinks: discard (irreversible per C-R6). 4-chapter vision expects 12 TOTAL weapon types — but 6-12 drops per chapter × 3 chapters = 18-36 weapons to choose 12 from. Math works but Pillar 3 'discover combos' relies on it.
issues.detail: weapon-ammo.md F1 + C-R8 + OQ-7 ALL note the cross-doc reconciliation need: 12 total weapon types in MVP, but level-dungeon.md F1 chapter 1 = 'A:1' (1 weapon) and 3-chapter VS = 4 weapons total. This was tagged as OQ-7 in level-dungeon.md. The fix is documented: VS adds more, MVP ships with chapter 1's 1 + a starting set. But the economic loop math isn't done: 23 slots × 12 weapon types = enough room, but if every encounter drops something (per tile re-trigger), the player will quickly fill the 20 backpack, and must discard (irreversible) or replace equipped. There's no 'sell for credits' or 'salvage for parts' documented. So weapons have no SINK except discard. Combined with infinite-source-from-replay, the weapon economy also runs away — but slower than ammo (3 slots cap is real, 20 backpack cap is real).
issues.recommendation: Add a small weapon sink: 'sell for credits (VS) OR discard for nothing (MVP) OR salvage for mech parts (VS)'. For MVP, document 'weapons have no currency value; discard = loss'. This is OK for MVP. The bigger issue: if the player gets 12 weapons and only 3 are equipped at a time, the Pillar 3 'discover the best combo' is bottlenecked by 'I have 12 options but 3 slots — I must TRY all 12 in some slots'. The Pillar 3 test passes if and only if the 12 weapons are genuinely distinct (each has a unique damage/accuracy/crit/special_effect signature). resource-data.md WeaponData has min_damage, max_damage, accuracy, crit_chance, crit_multiplier, special_effects, compatible_ammo_types — 7 dimensions. With 12 weapons × 3 ammo types = 36 unique build outcomes. That's plenty for Pillar 3. But the 12-weapon design needs to be confirmed by content authoring, not just GDD math.

issues.summary: Truth fragments: 4 per chapter, 1 source per fragment (or shared), 1 unlock per fragment (per C-R4). Clean one-way flow: source → unlock → story map. No sink, no runaway. Good.
issues.detail: npc-terminal.md F1: 4 fragments per chapter from 6 terminals + 1 NPC (with 2 terminals sharing 1 fragment). F4: 'unique_fragments_unlocked ≤ total_terminals + total_npcs' = upper-bounded. resource-data.md StoryFragmentData is immutable. save-load.md AC-18: 4 fragments persist across save/load. The fragment economy is: source-count bounded by level design (max 4 per chapter), 1:1 unlock, no duplicates (per C-R4 + E1 + OQ-2.2). This is the cleanest economic loop of the three. Pillar 4 is well-supported.
issues.recommendation: No action. The fragment economy is exemplary. Consider promoting this design as a template: 'bounded source + 1:1 unlock + immutable state + persist across save' = the right pattern for narrative collection systems. The npc-terminal.md F1 + C-R4 + F4 trio is the cleanest spec in the project. Other systems (ammo, weapons) should be benchmarked against this pattern.

issues.summary: HP / Mech economy: 4 parts × 100 HP = 400 max. Source: level exploration (repair_kit drops, ammo-pickup also drops repair_kit per battle-core-loop C-R5 priority 1). Sink: damage in battle. Auto-mode AI 'use repair_kit if HP≤30%' = perfect self-heal loop. No run-away if repair_kit is rate-limited.
issues.detail: resource-data.md MechPartData: 4 parts × 100 HP = 400 total. battle-core-loop.md F5: rewards include 'repair_kit' (item), drop rate depends on enemy type. Auto-mode C-R5 priority 1: 'use repair_kit if HP≤30%'. So auto-mode self-heals indefinitely (assuming repair_kit supply). The supply is bounded by drop rates (per encounter tile, per chest). Combined with encounter tile re-trigger, the player can farm repair_kits infinitely. But HP itself caps at 400 (4 parts × 100). So the HP economy is: source = repair_kit drops (rate-limited by encounter), sink = damage (rate-limited by encounter). Balance depends on tuning the two rates against each other. resource-data.md MechPartData has 'max_hp: 50-500' (production range, default 100), so designers have room. No Pillar test currently asserts the HP/repair balance.
issues.recommendation: Add a Pillar-3-style tuning test to resource-data.md Tuning Knobs: 'GIVEN encounter rate + repair_kit drop rate + auto-mode AI heal threshold, verify the player cannot trivially heal to full between every encounter (i.e., 1 fight = full HP cycle is too easy)'. Suggested target: 'average fight should bring HP to ~70% of max (small dip) — auto-heal is for emergencies, not for free wins'. If the math doesn't support this, lower the repair_kit drop rate or raise the auto-heal threshold from 30% to 50%.

issues.summary: Difficulty curve is single-chapter only (MVP scope), but the within-chapter curve has a known flat spot: 88% grunts for entire chapter means difficulty peaks early (first elite at room 7-8) and stays flat after
issues.detail: random-encounter.md F2: chapter 1 enemy distribution = 22 grunt (88%) + 3 elite (12%) + 0 boss. Where are those 3 elites? random-encounter.md doesn't say. level-dungeon.md F1 chapter 1 = 10 rooms. If elites are at rooms 8/9/10 (last 3), the curve is: rooms 1-7 = all grunts (flat 30-50 HP, 25 attack), rooms 8-10 = mix of grunts + elites (80-120 HP, ~40 attack). boss is room 10. So the 'difficulty ramp' is 1-step at room 7→8, then flat through room 9, then spike at room 10 (boss). That's not a curve, it's a cliff. resource-data.md F4 confirms boss 200-500 HP, 40-50 attack. The weapon-ammo.md F1 mid-game build (粒子炮 35 × 电浆 1.3 = 45 damage) vs grunt (40 HP) = 1-shot grunts, vs elite (100 HP) = 2-3 shots, vs boss (200-300 HP per battle-core-loop F4) = 5-7 shots. With 3-5 turns per elite and 8-15 turns per boss (battle-core-loop F4), the elite feels harder than the boss in terms of turns-per-HP. The boss needs the 'multi-phase' treatment flagged in battle-core-loop Rec #3 to feel different from a 200-HP elite.
Within-chapter difficulty curve is mostly flat with a late spike. Resource #1 confirms: 'BOSS 例外：HP 可达 200-500 但需要
或

等机制来扩展战斗长度'. Random-encounter OQ-3 + battle-core-loop Rec #3 both flag the same issue: BOSS as 'just more HP' is unsatisfying. Weapon-ammo.md F1 mid-game build deletes normal enemies (1-shot), so the chapter's first 7 rooms are also flat (no real challenge once player has 粒子炮). The 22 grunts aren't a 'progression' — they're a 'filler between encounter start and elite arrival'.
issues.recommendation: Add a within-chapter difficulty curve to level-dungeon.md or random-encounter.md: 'rooms 1-3 = tutorial grunts (15-25 HP, no elites) + locked doors; rooms 4-6 = mid grunts (30-50 HP) + 1 elite at room 5; rooms 7-9 = hard grunts (40-50 HP) + 2 elites (rooms 7, 9); room 10 = boss'. The 3 elites should be spaced (rooms 5, 7, 9), not bunched. Add a per-room enemy pool to EncounterTable (currently it's a flat list per chapter, not per-room). Confirm BOSS uses multi-phase per Rec #3 OR a clear 'this BOSS is a stat-check, not a mechanics-test' design. Without this, the chapter reads as: 'boring for 7 rooms, hard for 2 rooms, then BOSS' — not a curve.

issues.summary: Player damage scales linearly (20-80 base, ×0.5-2.0 ammo, ×1-3 crit) but enemy HP is fixed-tier (30-200, no in-tier scaling). The two scales don't talk to each other in any documented way
issues.detail: resource-data.md production range: WeaponData.min_damage 1-200 (tightened to 20-80 per Tuning Knobs), EnemyData.max_hp 10-500 (recommend 30-50 grunt, 80-120 elite, 200-500 boss). weapon-ammo.md F1: build_damage_preview 16-104. battle-core-loop.md F1: final_damage 8-480 (with weakness_mult + crit). But there's no 'level scaling' for enemies beyond the 3 tiers — no in-tier gradient. So a chapter 1 grunt (HP 30-50) is the SAME stat block as a chapter 3 grunt (HP 30-50) if designer just reuses EnemyData. The only difficulty mechanism is the enemy-tier mix (88% grunts chapter 1, 64% grunts chapter 3 per random-encounter F2). This means a player who finds 1-2 strong weapons early can flatten the entire MVP (since no enemy ever gets harder within the same tier). The 'level' system in game-concept 'Long-Term Progression' lists 'weapon library expansion' as #1, then 'mech upgrade' (not in MVP), then 'map knowledge' (chapter-locked), then 'story puzzle' (chapter-locked). So MVP has no horizontal difficulty progression beyond 'new weapons' and 'new chapters'.

Player damage has 4 dimensions of variance (base, ammo, crit, weakness), but enemy HP has 1 dimension (tier, fixed). The variance ratio is asymmetric: player can vary damage 3x-6x at the same tier (default build 20 vs optimal build 65), enemies can't vary. So 'build depth' is real (Pillar 3) but 'difficulty depth' is shallow. For a 1-3 month MVP, this is FINE — adding enemy-tier variance adds balance work. But the GDDs should explicitly say: 'within a tier, enemy HP is fixed; build is the player variable'.
issues.recommendation: Document the asymmetric scaling in game-concept or battle-core-loop: 'Enemy HP is fixed per tier (grunt/elite/boss); player damage is the variable. This means build experimentation is the primary difficulty modulator within a chapter; the chapter mix is the only between-chapter modulation'. This is a feature, not a bug — it aligns with Pillar 3 (build test) and Pillar 1 (encounter variety within a chapter). It also makes the '1-3 month MVP' scope feasible (no need to design 10+ enemy variants). Optionally, in VS: add 'elite-variant' and 'grunt-variant' EnemyData subtypes that share base stats but have different weakness/resistance/drops profiles — this gives VS chapters more variety without rebalancing.

issues.summary: All 12 GDDs declare pillar alignment; pillar drift is mostly absent; one mild concern: collision.md is Pillar 1+3 but Player Fantasy is 'foundation, not player-facing' which slightly drifts from pillar-mapped framing
issues.detail: Per the task brief's pillar mapping table, all 12 GDDs have stated pillars. Cross-check: game-state-machine claims 'Pillar 1+2+3+4 indirect' (the only GDD claiming all 4). resource-data 'P2+P3+P4' covers discovery/build/truth. player-input 'P1+P3+P4'. camera 'P1+P3'. collision 'P1+P3' but Player Fantasy says 'Foundation, not a player-facing system'. save-load 'P1+P4'. The mapping is complete and there are NO GDDs that don't map to a pillar. Pillar drift: ZERO. The closest thing to drift is collision.md: it claims P1+P3 (exploration density + build test) but its Player Fantasy section says 'the system is invisible — it's the foundation, not a player-facing system'. That's a deliberate disconnect (infrastructure doesn't have a player fantasy), not drift. The GDDs are well-pillar-aligned. Compare to a 'bad' set: a Pillar 3 GDD whose Player Fantasy is 'I love min-maxing damage numbers' (that's Pillar 2, not 3) — that would be drift. None of the 12 have this issue.

The GDDs collectively cover all 4 pillars multiple times, and no GDD claims a pillar it doesn't substantively serve. Pillar 1 (exploration density) is served by level-dungeon, random-encounter, camera, hud, save-load, player-input, collision. Pillar 2 (discovery) by resource-data, npc-terminal, weapon-ammo, battle-core-loop. Pillar 3 (build test) by player-input, camera, collision, battle-core-loop, weapon-ammo, random-encounter, resource-data. Pillar 4 (truth collection) by resource-data, player-input, npc-terminal, save-load. Overlap is healthy for an interconnected system; nothing is over- or under-served.
issues.recommendation: No action. Consider promoting the 'pillar mapping' pattern (every GDD has an 'Implements Pillar' header line) as a project standard. Add to design-docs.md: 'Every GDD must list which pillars it implements in its header'. The current 12 GDDs are the proof-of-concept.

issues.summary: Anti-pillar compliance is good (no permanent death = save-load Pillar 1+4, no open world = level-dungeon tile-based, no multiplayer = all GDDs single-player, no forced manual = battle-core-loop auto+manual); but the 'no forced manual' is undermined by the 3c-1 dominant-strategy issue
issues.detail: game-concept anti-pillars: NOT 开放世界, NOT 永久死亡, NOT 多人, NOT 强制手动. Cross-check: (a) 开放世界: level-dungeon.md C-R1 'MVP 1 chapter, hand-crafted tile-based' = compliant. (b) 永久死亡: save-load.md C-R1+C-R3 'autosave at safe points', battle-core-loop.md AC-17 'defeat → replace(TITLE)' — so death is punished (full restart) but progress is preserved via save. NOT permanent death. Compliant. (c) 多人: all GDDs single-player. Compliant. (d) 强制手动: battle-core-loop.md C-R4 'A 键模式切换不打断回合' + auto-mode AI per C-R5 + game-concept '自动攻击节奏' explicit. Compliant. So 4/4 anti-pillars are honored. The TENSION: anti-pillar 'no forced manual' is also one of the causes of 3c-1 (auto-mode trivializes Pillar 3). The anti-pillar is being followed; the side-effect is a pillar-undermining strategy. This is a real design tension that the GDDs don't acknowledge.

Anti-pillars are followed to the letter. The unintended consequence is that 3 of 4 pillars (build test, build experimentation, build depth) all rely on the player CHOOSING to engage with builds, and anti-pillar 'no forced manual' removes the leverage to require that engagement. Pillar 3 is the only one with this tension — Pillars 1, 2, 4 are not undermined by anti-pillars. The game-concept 'recovery from failure' section says '打不过 boss: 可以回去刷弹药 / 换 build, 没有
门槛' — endorsing the 'farm-and-swap' strategy that 3c-1 warns about.
issues.recommendation: In game-concept, add a paragraph to anti-pillar section: 'NOT 强制手动 does NOT mean
— it means

. Manual mode is a Pillar 3 first-class path; auto mode is a Pillar 1 (exploration pace) first-class path. The game's CHALLENGE is calibrated for manual mode engagement; auto mode is a difficulty-reducer. We do not enforce manual mode; we do not pretend auto mode is the same challenge.' This reframes 3c-1 as 'intentional design' rather than 'unintended loophole'. The Pillar 3 design test in game-concept already does this implicitly ('the boss can be defeated with normal ammo' → wrong), but it doesn't acknowledge auto-mode as a bypass.

issues.summary: Player fantasy is coherent: '孤独的考古学家/猎人 in a forgotten satellite, building a mech to discover truth' — exploration + collection + combat are all in service of discovery
issues.detail: game-concept.md Core Fantasy: '你不是在玩游戏, 你是在

'. Player Type Appeal: Explorers (core) + Achievers (secondary). Cross-check GDDs: (1) battle-core-loop Player Fantasy: '战斗从不卡顿 + 战术深度可自选' — supports 'archaeologist/hunter who fights with purpose'. (2) weapon-ammo Player Fantasy: '我有一把 arsenal, 每个 build 都是一个新发现' — supports 'mech pilot customizing tools'. (3) level-dungeon Player Fantasy: '我永远期待下一个房间' — supports 'archaeologist exploring ruins'. (4) npc-terminal Player Fantasy: '我拼出了真相的一部分' — supports 'archaeologist piecing together lost history'. (5) resource-data Player Fantasy: '玩家不会意识到这个系统' (implicit: 'data' supports discovery) — supports 'mech systems are tools, not the point'. These 4-5 identity frames are COMPATIBLE. The '孤独' (solitary) is consistent with single-player + no co-op + no NPCs-with-side-quests (1 NPC per chapter is decorative). The '考古学家/猎人' duality is the discoverer + the survivor. No conflict.

Cross-system fantasy coherence is high. The potential TENSION: 'mech pilot customizing tools' (weapon-ammo) vs 'archaeologist piecing together truth' (npc-terminal) — the pilot cares about the build, the archaeologist cares about the story. These are two simultaneous 'fantasy layers' for the same player. The game-concept 'Player Motivation Profile' lists Autonomy (weapon builds) + Competence (combat mastery) + Relatedness (civilization connection) as Core/Supporting — these are the 3 fantasy layers. They don't conflict; they coexist. The risk would be if ONE GDD made the player's identity incompatible with another (e.g., 'the player is a pacifist' + 'the player is a combat master'). No GDD does this.
issues.recommendation: Document the 'fantasy layers' explicitly in game-concept: 'Layer 1 (immediate, 30s) = mech pilot optimizing build; Layer 2 (short, 5-15min) = explorer mapping ruins; Layer 3 (long, 3-5hr) = archaeologist recovering lost civilization'. This is a refinement of the current 'Core Mechanics' list, not a contradiction. Each GDD's Player Fantasy can then reference its layer. The Pillar mapping also fits: P3 = Layer 1, P1 = Layer 2, P4 = Layer 3, P2 = 'the meta-layer that makes all 3 satisfying'.

issues.summary: Auto-mode fantasy ('I am a tactician watching my AI execute my optimal strategy') is internally coherent but doesn't have a single GDD Player Fantasy dedicated to it
issues.detail: battle-core-loop.md Player Fantasy covers BOTH modes: 'manual = tactical depth', 'auto = decompress / can grind while doing other things'. But the auto-mode fantasy is described in a subordinate clause, not as a peer to manual. The level-dungeon.md + random-encounter.md + hud.md GDDs all describe a player who is ACTIVELY engaged with the world (walking carefully, expecting encounters, reading HUD). The auto-mode player is PASSIVE (watching the AI, doing something else). These two 'while in battle' identities are not the same. Anti-pillar 'NOT 强制手动' endorses both, but no GDD says 'when in auto mode, the player's fantasy is X'.

The 3c-1 dominant-strategy issue is partly a fantasy-coherence issue: the design says 'manual = Pillar 3' but doesn't have a clear alternative fantasy for 'auto = ???'. Currently the auto-mode fantasy is implicit: 'I can chill while my mech does the work'. That's a valid fantasy (think idle games, AFK arena) but it doesn't get its own Player Fantasy section. If 3c-1 is fixed by option (2) 'add Pillar 5: Comfort', each GDD would need to mention 'Pillar 5 also served' for the auto-mode path. Otherwise the design treats auto-mode as a workaround rather than a first-class experience.
issues.recommendation: If 3c-1 is resolved by adding Pillar 5, update battle-core-loop.md Player Fantasy to have two subsections: 'Manual Mode Fantasy = tactical commander making build decisions', 'Auto Mode Fantasy = tactical commander watching the optimal strategy execute (the player IS still the strategist, the AI is the executor; the player is learning patterns, not absent)'. This is a small reframe that makes auto-mode a coherent Pillar 3 path. Alternative: don't add Pillar 5, and explicitly say in anti-pillar: 'auto mode is a difficulty-reducer, not a separate fantasy'.

scenarios_walked.name: Player walks on encounter tile during exploration
scenarios_walked.trigger: Player moves into ENCOUNTER layer area_entered signal fires
scenarios_walked.issue: OK with one note: 7 systems activate (collision signal → encounter manager → state machine transition → camera FADE_BLACK → battle init → HUD state badge update → encounter counter update). Latency budget: collision F1 (0.05-0.2ms) + encounter routing (C-R2, no formula) + state machine F1 (9-16.5ms total transition) + camera RIG_TRANSITION (0.4s fade) + battle INIT (immediate) + HUD per-frame (0.05-0.1ms state badge). Total: 9-17ms transition + 0.4s visual fade. Within budget. The note: encounter counter HUD update is per #16 'per-tile-unique' (pending #16 Rec #5 resolution). If the count includes re-triggers, the X/25 number doesn't increase past 25 even if player walks the same tile 100 times. If it doesn't, the number is honest but doesn't reflect 'I engaged with 100 fights'. This semantic gap needs HUD GDD resolution. Encounter tile re-trigger (per #16 C-R3) + infinite ammo source = the 3c-1 / 3d-1 dominant strategy opens here.

scenarios_walked.name: Player fires weapon in manual battle mode
scenarios_walked.issue: Heavy frame: 6 systems active simultaneously. Per 3b-1, this is the highest-cognitive-load moment. Sequence: (1) InputBus routes 1/2/3 action per #2 C-R4 + battle-core-loop C-R3 (immediate attack, no confirm). (2) weapon-ammo C-R3: active weapon is the slot, current ammo is global. (3) battle-core-loop PHASE_PLAYER_ACTION executes attack. (4) resource-data / DamageCalc: final_damage = base × ammo_mult × crit_mult × weakness_mult × defense_mult. (5) camera F1: 4px shake / 100ms (instant on hit). (6) HUD F2 damage number: 0.5s float. (7) HUD F4 weapon slot pulse animation: 0.5Hz continues. (8) HUD weapon icon highlight per #2 G-H-3: 0.1s highlight. (9) battle-state push to HUD per #7 UI Requirements: every frame. Latency: input-to-handler 200-500µs + handler-to-visual 8-16ms = 8.2-16.5ms per #2 F1. Frame budget 16.6ms — at the edge. 3b-1 is the relevant issue. The 3c-2 damage-bound mismatch (battle-core-loop F1 says 8-480, weapon-ammo F1 says 16-104, resource-data ceiling says 8-1200) becomes visible HERE: the player sees a damage number and may not know if it's the max possible.

scenarios_walked.name: Player saves in exploration, then loads
scenarios_walked.trigger: Player presses F5 in EXPLORATION state, then later quits and restarts, choosing 'Continue' from TITLE
scenarios_walked.issue: 10 systems involved; load is the heaviest single-frame operation in the game. Save side: F5 in EXPLORATION is valid per save-load AC-6. SaveManager calls get_state_snapshot() on each system (10 systems, ~2ms each per F4) = 20ms + JSON encode 2ms + file write 5ms + flush 3ms = ~30ms async. Per C-R6 async doesn't block; player keeps playing. Load side: per F4 = 32ms sync (deferred to loading screen) = JSON decode 2ms + state_restore 20ms + UI rebuild 10ms. This is the 500ms loading fade. Within budget. The 3c-3 save-scum concern is the only real issue: the player can F5 before any encounter, F9 to retry. With 3 manual slots + 1 autosave, that's 3 'rewinds' per chapter. Acceptable. The cross-doc OQ-7 (level-dungeon weapon count vs weapon-ammo TOTAL_WEAPON_TYPES_AVAILABLE = 12) becomes visible HERE: a load may reference a weapon ID that doesn't exist in the current .tres set. Per save-load E8 + resource-data AC-14, the load self-heals (null in slot + HUD prompt). The cross-doc alignment is RECOMMENDED but not blocking.

scenarios_walked.name: Player reads terminal and gets a fragment
scenarios_walked.trigger: Player walks into INTERACTABLE terminal area, presses E, push(TERMINAL) state, listens to audio, fragment unlocks
scenarios_walked.issue: OK with one note: 8 systems activate. The terminal interaction is the cleanest spec in the project. Push(TERMINAL) per #3 → camera RIG_TERMINAL_CLOSEUP per #4 → terminal UI shows per #18 → audio plays per #18 F2 (60s default) → fragment unlocks per C-R3 (emit story_fragment_unlocked) → HUD fragment popup → save-load serialization. Latency: state push 9-16.5ms + camera zoom 600ms (intentional, '剧照' effect) + audio duration 60s = 60s player experience, no frame-budget concerns. The note: 3d-3 (fragment economy) is exemplary. Pillar 4 is the cleanest-served pillar. The 2-terminals-sharing-1-fragment (per #18 F1) is a Pillar 4 design choice ('any path can unlock truth') that's been flagged as OQ-2.2 (C-R6 needed). If resolved as 'shared fragment = unlock on first hit' (current default per F1), no gameplay issue. If resolved as '1:1 strict', #18 F1 + HUD AC-18 numbers change. Recommend resolve in this Phase 3 review rather than defer to implementation.

scenarios_walked.name: Player picks up a new weapon in battle loot
scenarios_walked.trigger: Battle ends in victory, rewards Dictionary emitted, weapon drop passes drop_rate check, popup appears
scenarios_walked.issue: Clean but a known risk: 5 systems, low frame cost. Sequence: (1) battle-core-loop emits battle_ended(victory, rewards) per C-R7. (2) weapon-ammo C-R5: emit weapon_pickup_offered(weapon). (3) HUD renders popup with 4 options (per #11+#12 F4 + #2 HUD UI-1). (4) Player decides (0.5s timeout = default to inventory per F4). (5) save-load autosave fires per C-R3. (6) If 'discover' = first time, resource-data + codex + HUD show 'new discovery'. Frame cost: 5 systems but no per-frame push (event-driven). The risk flagged in weapon-ammo Rec #3: '1/2/3 = 装备/入背包/丢弃 buttons in popup BUT 1/2/3 in battle = immediate attack' — keybinding collision. If popup appears during BATTLE state (which it shouldn't per the OQ resolution: 'only BATTLE_END_VICTORY or EXPLORATION'), and player presses 1/2/3 thinking it's battle, they accidentally equip/discard. weapon-ammo OQ-3 is the right place to resolve this; the answer is the popup should be UNABLE to appear in BATTLE per state stack. Recommend explicit AC: 'popup only in BATTLE_END_VICTORY or EXPLORATION' and 'in BATTLE_END_VICTORY, popup blocks transition to EXPLORATION until decision made'.
